resources.note: Perpetual free tier ~1M chars/month Neural2 and ~4M chars/month WaveNet; then ~$16/M (Neural2), ~$4/M (WaveNet). Needs a GCP account + API key.

resources.name: gTTS documentation (low-quality free fallback)
resources.note: Free Python TTS via unofficial Google Translate endpoint. Flatter Russian quality, undocumented rate limits, history of chunking bugs for Russian. Use only as last resort.

resources.name: OpenAI Text-to-Speech guide & models
resources.note: tts-1 ~$15/M chars; gpt-4o-mini-tts ~1.5 cents/min. Russian supported but voices are English-optimized (accent risk); no phoneme/SSML stress control.

resources.name: ElevenLabs Text-to-Speech (premium, limited free tier)
resources.note: Most natural Russian voices; free tier only ~10k chars/month (~10 min); paid from $5/mo. Best for a few showcase clips, not bulk SRS audio.

resources.name: MDN - SpeechSynthesisVoice (localService property)
resources.note: Authoritative: localService boolean distinguishes on-device (offline) vs remote (network) voices; available voice set is whatever the OS/browser provides.

resources.name: Readium 'SpeechSynthesis in browsers and OSes' survey
resources.note: Detailed cross-browser/OS audit: Chrome ~19 online-only voices, Edge 250+ online-only neural voices, Safari hides high-quality voices, Firefox uses OS voices; documents naming/quality inconsistency.

resources.name: Edge TTS voice list (FoloToy wiki)
resources.note: Confirms the ru-RU edge-tts voices: DmitryNeural (M) and SvetlanaNeural (F).

uncertainties: Exact, current edge-tts rate-limit thresholds and long-term reliability are unknown because it relies on an undocumented Microsoft endpoint; it has broken before when Microsoft changed the service. Caching and a Google Cloud fallback mitigate this but the risk is real and not contractually covered.

I did not run a side-by-side listening test of ru-RU-SvetlanaNeural vs Google ru-RU Neural2 vs ElevenLabs Russian; relative naturalness rankings are drawn from documentation, MOS figures, and aggregated reviews rather than my own A/B evaluation of Russian-specific stress/palatalization. A quick personal listening test on your actual target sentences is advisable before locking the default.

I could not confirm the precise set and quality of Russian voices the user's specific macOS version exposes to the Web Speech API (it varies by OS version and which voices are downloaded); the 'robotic on macOS' assessment is based on the general nature of Apple's API-exposed Russian voices, not a check of this exact machine.

OpenAI/ElevenLabs/Google pricing and free-tier numbers were gathered from current (June 2026) sources but vendor pricing and free-tier limits change; verify the exact current figures on the official pages before committing budget.
gTTS character/hour rate-limit figures come from community reports, not official documentation (Google publishes no quota for this unofficial endpoint), so treat them as approximate.

verdicts.explanation: SUPPORTED. Nation 2006 confirms 98 percent equals 1 in 50 and 95 percent equals 1 in 20; Laufer 2010 gives 98 optimal and 95 minimal; Schmitt 2011 found linear and endorsed 98 percent.

verdicts.claim: FSRS is current state-of-the-art and Anki's default since v23.10 (Nov 2023); uses a three-component DSR model vs SM-2's single ease factor; on the open-spaced-repetition benchmark (~727M reviews/~10k users) beats SM-2 for ~99.5% of users and needs ~20-30% fewer reviews for the same retention. Recommendation: build on py-fsrs (pip install fsrs, import name fsrs), wrap Scheduler/Card/Rating/review_card in a thin service, persist full Card FSRS state + every ReviewLog, and do not implement SM-2 yourself.
verdicts.explanation:
Almost every part of the claim checks out, but one decision-critical fact is wrong: FSRS is NOT and was NOT "Anki's default since v23.10."

WHAT IS WRONG — the "default since 23.10" attribution:
- The official Anki 23.10 changelog says FSRS support was "now integrated into Anki" — i.e. it became available and could be configured inside Anki without custom-scheduling hacks. It does NOT say it became the default. (changes.ankiweb.net/changes/23.10.html)
- The official Anki manual (deck-options.html) still frames FSRS as opt-in: "When you turn on FSRS..." and "Enable FSRS under the 'FSRS' section, at the bottom of the deck options page." This is the language of a feature you must switch on, not a default.
- The official Anki FAQ (faqs.ankiweb.net/what-spaced-repetition-algorithm) lists SM-2 first and FSRS second as two available algorithms, again not declaring FSRS the default.
- Anki's lead developer (dae) explicitly wrote in a Jan 29, 2024 forum thread titled "Why isn't FSRS the default scheduler?" that it is not the default yet, citing integration kinks and users who fail cards with Hard instead of Again. So as of early 2024 SM-2 was still the default for new collections, with FSRS opt-in. (Note: secondary blog/SEO sites do loosely claim "default since 23.10," but they are not authoritative and conflict with Anki's own docs and developer.) The correct statement is: 23.10 (Nov 2023) is when FSRS became a built-in, officially supported, opt-in option in Anki — not the default.

WHAT IS CORRECT (well-supported):
- State of the art / DSR model: FSRS uses the three-component DSR model — Difficulty, Stability, Retrievability — vs SM-2's single ease factor. Confirmed by the official FSRS/DSR repo and the algorithm write-ups. A notable practical upside: Difficulty uses mean reversion, avoiding SM-2's "ease hell."
- Benchmark accuracy: The open-spaced-repetition srs-benchmark uses ~727M reviews / ~10k users (with ~350M reviews used for evaluation after excluding same-day reviews). The official benchmark page reports FSRS-6 superiority over Anki SM-2 of ~99.6% of collections (the ~99.5% figure is accurate). One fair caveat the benchmark itself flags: SM-2 was not designed to output recall probabilities, so probability-conversion formulas were bolted on for comparison — the comparison is not perfectly apples-to-apples, though it is the standard cited evidence.
- ~20-30% fewer reviews for the same retention: Widely cited and attributed to the Anki team's own testing and large-scale benchmarks. This is the one quantitative claim with weaker primary sourcing (it appears in secondary write-ups and Anki team statements rather than a single rigorous primary paper in the benchmark repo), but it is the consensus figure and I found nothing refuting it. Treat it as a reasonable planning estimate, not a guaranteed result for a from-zero personal app with little data.

THE py-fsrs RECOMMENDATION (sanity-check) — sound:
- pip install fsrs is correct; import name is fsrs; classes Scheduler, Card, Rating, ReviewLog exist; the workflow card, review_log = scheduler.review_card(card, rating) is the documented API. The package is actively maintained (latest v6.3.1, March 2026; ~36 releases).
- It exposes ReviewLog and an optional optimizer via pip install "fsrs[optimizer]" that computes optimal parameters/retention from historical ReviewLogs — so the advice to persist every ReviewLog for later optimization is correct and important (the optimizer is useless without stored logs).
- Card scheduling state (stability, difficulty, due, last_review, step, state) is tracked on the Card object; Card supports serialization, so persisting full state is straightforward. Wrapping the library behind a thin scheduling service is good design and keeps you insulated from FSRS-6 -> future-version changes.
- "Do NOT implement SM-2 yourself; FSRS is strictly better and the library handles the math" — directionally fine for a personal app, but two caveats: (1) "strictly better" is an overstatement — FSRS's edge depends on rating Hard vs Again consistently, which is the exact reason Anki's dev kept it opt-in; for a from-zero learner this is a real usability consideration. (2) With near-zero review history, FSRS runs on default/global parameters and only personalizes after you accumulate enough reviews to run the optimizer — so early on the practical accuracy gap over SM-2 is smaller than the headline benchmark numbers suggest.

BOTTOM LINE: Build on py-fsrs as recommended — that guidance is solid. But correct the one load-bearing fact in your design doc: FSRS has been a built-in opt-in option in Anki since v23.10 (Nov 2023); it was not Anki's default at that time (SM-2 was). Hence verdict = mixed rather than supported.

verdicts.claim: The dominant pedagogical model groups the 33 Cyrillic letters by visual/phonetic relationship to Latin (not alphabetically) in a four-bucket sequence — (1) true friends, (2) false friends, (3) unfamiliar shape/familiar sound, (4) genuinely new letters/sounds + the two signs — and Babbel, RussianLessons.net, the University of Arizona CMES module, and Polyglottist all converge on this same sequence. Plus a recommended lesson split with specific letters per bucket.
verdicts.explanation:
CORE MODEL: STRONGLY SUPPORTED. The four-bucket sequence (true friends to false friends to unfamiliar-shape/familiar-sound to new sounds + signs) is genuinely the dominant beginner-facing model for English speakers, used across many independent sources. It originates with RussianPod101's "True/False/New Friends + Strangers" series and is mirrored by RussianLessons.net, Polyglottist Language Academy, Rocket Languages, Fluent in 3 Months, and others. The first three buckets reproduce almost identically everywhere (True: А К М О Т; False: В Е Н Р С У Х; New friends: Б Г Д З И Л П Ф Э). So the pedagogical thrust of the takeaway holds.

SOURCE-CONVERGENCE CLAIM: PARTIALLY REFUTED. The assertion that "Babbel ... and the University of Arizona CMES academic module ... all converge on this same four-bucket sequence" does not hold up to two of its four named sources.
 - Babbel: I could not confirm Babbel uses this four-bucket model. Babbel's public alphabet pages (babbel.com/russian-alphabet, the "Cyrillic in two days" magazine piece) discuss history and mention isolated false-friend examples but do NOT present the structured four-bucket sequence. Whatever is inside Babbel's paywalled course is unverifiable from public sources; citing Babbel as a converging authority for THIS specific schema is unsupported.
 - "University of Arizona CMES academic module": I could not locate any such module. "CMES" is Center for Middle Eastern Studies — wrong center for Russian (UArizona Russian sits under the Critical Languages Program / Russian & Slavic dept, and its historical Critical Languages Series did not even cover Russian). This citation appears to be fabricated or misremembered; treat it as non-existent until a real URL is produced.
 - Only RussianLessons.net and Polyglottist are verifiably accurate citations for the four-bucket sequence.

RECOMMENDATION LETTER ASSIGNMENTS: ONE REAL ERROR, otherwise sound.
 - Lesson 1 (А К М О Т) and Lesson 2 (В Н Р С У Х + Е) match the canonical model exactly — Е is correctly a false friend. Good.
 - Lesson 3 PROBLEM: it places the iotated vowels Ё Ю Я (and lists Й in L3) into the "unfamiliar-shape/familiar-sound" bucket. This contradicts the dominant model it claims to follow. RussianPod101's canonical taxonomy puts Ё Ю Я Й, the hissing letters, Ы, and both signs ALL in the final "Strangers" group — Й, Ё, Ю, Я are explicitly classed as Strangers, not new-friends. Pedagogically the iotated vowels carry a genuinely new sound/behavior (preceding /j/, palatalization) so they belong in bucket 4, not bucket 3. The recommendation also drops Й inconsistently (named in L3 but also implied in L4). Minor count note: with Е moved to L2, buckets 1–3 hold 5+7+ letters and the totals only reach 33 if the iotated vowels land in L4. Recommend moving Ё Ю Я Й to Lesson 4.

OTHER SANITY CHECKS (supported): recognition-before-production and time-to-read framing are well attested — sources report basic Cyrillic decoding in a few days to ~2 weeks, with transliteration deliberately removed after ~7–10 days to force direct reading, which aligns with the app's SRS + comprehensible-input design.

verdicts.claim: STRESS is the keystone problem of Russian for from-zero English speakers: Russian stress is lexically unpredictable and mobile across a word's inflectional paradigm, phonemic in some pairs, with no reliable rule set, so it must be learned per lexeme and per form. Therefore stress should be modeled as a first-class per-word-FORM attribute (stored on lemma AND each inflected form, with a mobile/stress-shift flag, as StimulStat exposes) and marked in all learner-facing Russian from day one, because vowel reduction is unrecoverable without it.
verdicts.explanation:
I tried to refute this and could not; every load-bearing component holds up under current scholarly and pedagogical sources.

CORE CLAIM (well supported):
- Unpredictable/lexical: Russian has a "free lexical stress system" where any syllable can be stressed; the Cambridge Phonology default-stress study concludes stress is "fundamentally lexical-dependent" with rule-based defaults only a supplementary fallback. Jouravlev & Lupker (2015) explicitly title Russian "an unpredictable stress language."
- Mobile across the paradigm: Confirmed — stress alternates between stem and suffix across inflectional forms; Zaliznyak's Grammatical Dictionary codifies 6 noun stress patterns (plus adjective/verb variants) precisely because stress shifts per form.
- Phonemic/contrastive: Confirmed via minimal pairs (muka 'torture' vs muka 'flour'; dorog vs dorog).
- Vowel reduction unrecoverable without stress: Strongly confirmed — reduction (akanye) is "entirely predictable once you know where the stress falls," and undecidable without it (canonical moloko example: of three letters O only the stressed one is clear).
- Marking stress in all learner-facing material from day one: Confirmed as standard pedagogy — learner dictionaries and textbooks mark stress for exactly this reason; native text omits it.

ONE NUANCE (does not overturn the takeaway): The very papers cited (Jouravlev & Lupker) found weak PROBABILISTIC non-lexical cues (onset/coda complexity, certain orthographic segments) that beat chance. So Russian stress is not literally rule-free; rather, no cue set is reliable enough to replace per-lexeme/per-form learning. The claim's phrase "no reliable rule set" is therefore accurate in spirit (no rules reliable enough to skip lexical storage) but slightly overstated if read as "no statistical regularities exist."

ONE PARTIALLY-VERIFIED DETAIL: StimulStat (52k+ lemmas, 1.7M+ word forms, 70+ properties) does store main/secondary stress position per lemma AND per word form — confirmed. I could NOT independently confirm it exposes an explicit boolean "mobile/stress-shift" flag (official site uses a self-signed cert; the Behavior Research Methods paper is paywalled). However, the data-model recommendation stands regardless: Zaliznyak-based resources (Wiktionary appendices, libraries like tsnorm) encode stress-shift paradigms per form, so storing stress per form plus a mobility flag is well-founded and is the right design. Recommend sourcing stress data from Zaliznyak/Wiktionary-derived datasets and verifying StimulStat's exact field list directly if it is to be cited as the model.

Bottom line: the decision-critical takeaway and the data-model recommendation are sound design drivers; treat the StimulStat "mobile flag exists" specific as unconfirmed and the "no rules at all" wording as a slight simplification.

verdicts.claim: hermitdave/FrequencyWords on GitHub provides OpenSubtitles-2018-derived Russian frequency lists at content/2018/ru/ (ru_50k.txt, ru_full.txt) as plain 'word count' text (e.g. 'я 5081568', 'не 4334804', 'что 3552532'), licensed MIT (code) + CC BY-SA 4.0 (data) with commercial use allowed under attribution + share-alike; recommended as the master frequency spine for a Russian-learning app.
verdicts.explanation:
The factual and technical core of the claim is verified exactly, but the license/commercial-use conclusion is overstated and partly inaccurate, so the takeaway is mixed rather than fully supported.

VERIFIED EXACTLY:
- The repo exists and is OpenSubtitles-2018-derived. README states the 2018 lists are generated from OpenSubtitles2018 (opus.nlpl.eu/OpenSubtitles2018).
- Path content/2018/ru/ exists and contains ru_50k.txt and ru_full.txt (plus ru_ignored.txt).
- Format is plain '{word}{space}{occurrence_count}' text, exactly as claimed.
- The example lines are verbatim-correct: I fetched the raw ru_50k.txt and its first three lines are 'я 5081568', 'не 4334804', 'что 3552532'.
- The README literally states 'MIT License for code. CC-by-sa-4.0 for content.'

PROBLEMS WITH THE LICENSE/REUSABILITY CLAIM (why this is not 'fully reusable, commercial use allowed'):
1. Repo LICENSE file mismatch: The repository's actual LICENSE file is MIT only (Copyright (c) 2016 Hermit Dave) and does not contain any CC-BY-SA text. The CC-BY-SA-4.0 designation lives only in the README prose / GitHub metadata, not in a formal license file applied to the data files. This weakens the clean 'dual license' framing.
2. Version inconsistency: The author's own canonical page (invokeit.wordpress.com/frequency-word-lists) states the lists are under Creative Commons Attribution/ShareAlike 3.0, not 4.0. So the author has published two different CC BY-SA versions (3.0 on his site, 4.0 on GitHub). The specific '4.0' in the claim is only one of two author-stated versions.
3. Upstream non-commercial restriction (the decision-critical risk): The data is derived from the OPUS OpenSubtitles2018 corpus, which OPUS explicitly does NOT own ('We do not own any of the text... We only offer files that we believe we are free to redistribute'). The underlying source, opensubtitles.org, restricts use to non-commercial, scientific and educational purposes. A downstream re-publisher's CC BY-SA label cannot grant broader rights than the upstream source allows, so the flat assertion 'commercial use allowed' is legally shaky for the data and should not be relied upon for a commercial app without independent legal review. Note also the data is frequency counts (facts), which in many jurisdictions are not themselves copyrightable — a separate argument that cuts toward reusability but is not what the claim asserts.

PRACTICAL RECOMMENDATION (the second part) is sound: ru_50k.txt is exactly a ranked 'word count' file; parsing lines into (rank, lemma, count) is trivial and correct (rank = line order, descending count). Using it as a frequency spine for sequencing vocabulary and weighting comprehensible-input sentence selection by unknown-word rank is a reasonable, common design. One technical caveat: the entries are surface word forms/tokens, not lemmas — Russian is heavily inflected, so 'lemma' in the parse tuple is a misnomer; you'd need lemmatization (e.g. pymorphy3/pymystem3) to get true lemma frequencies, or accept form-level frequencies.

NET: Format, path, files, example data, and the MIT-code/CC-BY-SA-content README labeling are all confirmed (supported). The 'CC BY-SA 4.0 + commercial use allowed' reusability conclusion is unreliable because of the LICENSE-file/version inconsistencies and, more importantly, the non-commercial restriction on the upstream OpenSubtitles source (refuted/uncertain). Recommend treating hermitdave as the best practical free Russian frequency list technically, but do NOT assume clean commercial rights to the data without legal review; consider the wordfreq Python package (which also draws on OpenSubtitles among other sources) as a cross-check.

verdicts.claim: Browser Web Speech API gives inconsistent/unreliable Russian coverage across browsers and OSes (per readium.org: Chrome ~19 online-only voices/15 langs; Edge 250+ online-only neural voices/75 langs; Safari hides downloadable voices and upgrading one can make it vanish; Firefox ships no native voices, falls back to OS), so it cannot be a primary engine. Recommendation: use edge-tts in a Python backend with ru-RU-SvetlanaNeural + ru-RU-DmitryNeural — free, no API key, high neural quality, exposes word boundaries for word-highlighting, alternate male/female voices.
verdicts.explanation:
I tried to refute this and could not; primary sources confirm it almost verbatim, with one caveat the user should plan around.

READIUM SURVEY (all confirmed near-verbatim at readium.org/speech/docs/WebSpeech.html):
- Chrome: "preloaded with a limited selection of 19 high quality voices across 15 languages" and "All of these voices require online access to use them, without any fallback to a lower quality offline variant." Matches "~19 / 15 languages / online-only."
- Edge: "over 250 preloaded voices across 75 languages (as of April 2024)" and these "natural" voices "rely on Machine Learning (ML) and therefore require online access." Matches "250+ / 75 languages / online-only." (Note a documented macOS quirk: Edge shows only ~18 voices until the API has spoken once, then expands to 250+ — which itself reinforces the "inconsistent/unreliable to rely on" thesis.)
- Safari: "Downloadable voices do not show up in the list returned by the Web Speech API," and "when installing higher quality variants of preloaded voices, these voices disappear in Safari, which means that entire languages could disappear completely." Matches the claim exactly.
- Firefox: "Firefox doesn't come with any preloaded voice of its own" and relies on system voices. Matches "no native voices, falls back to OS."
The overarching conclusion (Web Speech API is too inconsistent across browser/OS to be a reliable primary engine, especially for a single non-English language like Russian) is well-supported by this survey, which is exactly why readium built a curated voice-ranking layer on top of it.

EDGE-TTS RECOMMENDATION (confirmed against the rany2/edge-tts repo):
- Free, no API key, no Edge/Windows needed: project tagline is "Use Microsoft Edge's online text-to-speech service from Python WITHOUT needing Microsoft Edge or Windows or an API key." Correct.
- Russian voices exist: ru-RU-SvetlanaNeural (Female) and ru-RU-DmitryNeural (Male) are both in the current voice list. Correct, and alternating them gives the male/female contrast claimed.
- Word boundaries for highlighting: CONFIRMED in source (communicate.py). Communicate.stream() yields dicts of {"type": "WordBoundary"/"SentenceBoundary", "offset", "duration", "text"} interleaved with {"type": "audio", "data": ...}. These offsets are real word-level timestamps and are exactly what SubMaker uses to build SRT/VTT subtitles, so they can drive current-word highlighting during comprehensible-input playback. Correct and load-bearing — this is the strongest reason to prefer edge-tts over browser TTS for a karaoke-style reader.
- High neural quality: these are Microsoft Azure-grade neural voices (same tech as Edge Read Aloud); quality is widely regarded as good for Russian. Reasonable.

CAVEATS the user must weigh (these prevent a blind "all-green" but do not refute the claim for THIS use case):
1) RELIABILITY: edge-tts is an unofficial, reverse-engineered client of Microsoft's Read Aloud endpoint, not a supported API. It has broken before (403 errors requiring a new Sec-MS-GEC anti-abuse token, GitHub issues #290/#458) and Microsoft has tightened filtering on datacenter/cloud IP ranges — so it can fail from servers on cloud IPs. For a LOCAL, personal app running from a residential IP this is low-risk and the package has historically been patched quickly, but a "default engine" should have a fallback path. Caching generated MP3s also sidesteps rate-limiting and latency.
2) TERMS / COMMERCIAL USE: Using Microsoft's service this way is generally treated as fine for personal/local use, but commercial use without an Azure subscription may violate Microsoft's ToS. The user's stated scope (local, personal, low-cost) fits the accepted use case; if it ever ships commercially, move to paid Azure TTS (same voices, same WordBoundary support) — a clean upgrade path.

Net: the refutation attempt fails. The factual claims are accurate, the recommendation is sound for a local personal Russian-learning app, and the only adjustment is to add a fallback engine (e.g. offline Piper/Silero, or paid Azure) and cache audio for robustness rather than treating the reverse-engineered endpoint as guaranteed-available.
